title: Communicating with Data: Charts That Drive Decisions

blocks.fintech_hook: When McKinsey studied how C-suite executives make decisions, they found that data presented visually was 43% more persuasive than the same data in table form. In private equity, the analyst who can turn a 200-row Excel model into a single chart that instantly shows why EBITDA margin expansion drives returns — rather than describing it in three paragraphs — wins the room. The chart is never the afterthought; in high-stakes finance, the chart is the argument.
blocks.explanation:
Data visualization is fundamentally a communication problem, not a technical one. The question is never 'how do I make this chart?' — it's 'what decision am I trying to support, and what visual form makes the answer impossible to miss?' Most business analysts default to bar charts and line charts for everything, producing decks full of slides that require five minutes of explanation. A well-designed chart communicates its insight before the presenter opens their mouth.

The discipline is matching the chart type to the relationship you're showing. Time series data belongs on line charts. Comparisons across categories belong on horizontal bar charts (easier to read than vertical when labels are long). Distributions belong on histograms or box plots — essential in risk management to show the shape of loss distributions. Correlations belong on scatter plots. Part-to-whole relationships belong on stacked bars or treemaps, almost never on pie charts, which are notoriously hard for humans to interpret accurately. In finance and FinTech, you'll often need waterfall charts to show how a starting value changes across contributing factors (a revenue bridge from one quarter to the next, or an LBO returns attribution showing multiple expansion versus EBITDA growth versus leverage).

The visual design choices that most affect comprehension are: color (use it sparingly and semantically — red means bad, green means good, grey is neutral background), annotation (the best charts label the key data point directly rather than relying on a legend the reader has to decode), and ruthless removal of anything that doesn't carry information. Removing gridlines, tick marks, and borders that add no data almost always makes a chart more readable. These principles apply whether you're building in Python with matplotlib, deploying an interactive dashboard in Plotly, or making a slide in PowerPoint.

code_example.code:
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Monthly revenue data for a hypothetical lending platform
data = {
    'month': ['Jan', 'Feb', 'Mar', 'Apr', 'May', 'Jun'],
    'revenue_m': [2.1, 2.4, 2.2, 2.8, 3.1, 3.5],
    'defaults_pct': [1.8, 2.0, 2.3, 1.9, 1.7, 1.6]
}
df = pd.DataFrame(data)

fig, ax1 = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5))

# Revenue bars on primary y-axis
ax1.bar(df['month'], df['revenue_m'], color='steelblue', alpha=0.75, label='Revenue ($M)')
ax1.set_ylabel('Revenue ($M)', color='steelblue')
ax1.set_ylim(0, 4.5)

# Default rate line on secondary y-axis
ax2 = ax1.twinx()
ax2.plot(df['month'], df['defaults_pct'], color='firebrick',
         marker='o', linewidth=2.5, label='Default Rate (%)')
ax2.set_ylabel('Default Rate (%)', color='firebrick')
ax2.set_ylim(0, 4)

# Annotate the key insight directly on the chart
ax2.annotate('Default rate falling\nas revenue grows',
             xy=('Jun', 1.6), xytext=('Mar', 0.6),
             arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color='black'), fontsize=9)

plt.title('Revenue vs. Default Rate — Lending Platform H1 2024', pad=12)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('revenue_vs_defaults.png', dpi=150)
print('Chart saved: revenue_vs_defaults.png')
